analyst_observations: Iteration 2: 49/49 (100%) with_skill vs 4/49 (8%) baseline — perfect pass rate maintained after substantially tightening the assertion set (16-17 per eval vs 8-9 in iter-1).

New assertions (environment, constraints, positionality, stock/flow, element_count_le_5, delay labels, non-emergent contrast, maintenance mechanism, failure-leverage pairing) all pass on every skill run — the integrations land cleanly without overloading the model.
Iter-1 → iter-2 comparison: assertion count rose from 25 → 49 while skill pass rate stayed at 100%. That means the richer skill successfully carries more structural requirements without degradation.

Cost increased: +30s (94s vs 64s) and +10k tokens (+39%). Time cost grew because of three new required sections (constraints, environment, positionality) plus tighter per-relation annotations (delay+polarity+reason). Worth it given 11.5× pass-rate gap.

Baseline pass rate fell (16% → 8%) because the new assertions raise the bar — baseline was already weak on original criteria, and now additionally fails all new ones (environment, constraints, stock/flow, delay labels, maintenance mechanism, leverage pairing). Only structural instinct it retains is observer-effect acknowledgement in meta eval.
No non-discriminating assertions — every added criterion splits the two configs.
Meta eval shows skill's largest gap: 17/17 vs 1/17. Self-reference handling, boundary-as-chosen-cut, and positionality remain uniquely skill-specific behaviors.

Element count limit (≤5) is strictly satisfied in all 3 skill runs, often with explicit commentary (eval 2 notes why 사전 개입 규칙 was absorbed into constraints rather than added as a 6th element). The discipline is visible in the output.
Anchor column hidden-by-default: all 3 skill runs omit the anchor column entirely. Abstraction stays clean without the user having to ask.
